deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 30-year-old male who's presenting with epigastric pain radiating to the back that wakes him up at night.
He's had similar pain before, which was diagnosed as a perforated duodenal ulcer and treated with an omental patch surgery twice.
His pain was controlled with proton pump inhibitors and analgesics.
First, the key symptoms here are epigastric pain that radiates to the back, which is a classic presentation for duodenal ulcers.
The fact that he's had this pain before and was diagnosed with a perforated duodenal ulcer is a big clue.
He's had surgery with an omental patch, which is a common procedure for repairing duodenal ulcers that have perforated.
The pain waking him up at night is also typical for peptic ulcers because stomach acid can build up during the night, causing irritation.
He's been treated with proton pump inhibitors, which are the standard treatment for ulcers, so that aligns with the history.

I'm considering if there could be any other conditions that present similarly, like a hiatal hernia or GERD, but the history of perforated ulcers and the need for surgery points more towards a duodenal ulcer.
The radiating pain to the back could also be a sign of referred pain from the duodenum.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is a duodenal ulcer, specifically a recurrent one given the history of two surgeries.
